name: Long Call
description: Buy a call option, giving you the right to purchase stock at the strike price. You profit when the stock rises above the strike plus the premium you paid. Your maximum loss is limited to the premium — you can never lose more than what you paid.
idealCondition: You expect the stock to rise significantly before expiration

name: Long Put
description: Buy a put option, giving you the right to sell stock at the strike price. You profit when the stock falls below the strike minus the premium. Think of it as insurance — you're betting the stock will drop.
idealCondition: You expect the stock to decline significantly before expiration

name: Short Call (Naked)
description: Sell a call option without owning the stock. You collect the premium upfront and profit if the stock stays below the strike. WARNING: This has unlimited risk — if the stock skyrockets, your losses are theoretically infinite.
idealCondition: You're confident the stock won't rise above the strike

name: Short Put (Naked)
description: Sell a put option. You collect the premium and profit if the stock stays above the strike. If the stock drops, you may be forced to buy it at the strike price. Risk is substantial but capped (stock can only fall to $0).
idealCondition: You're bullish and willing to buy the stock at the strike price

name: Covered Call
description: Own 100 shares of stock and sell a call against them. You collect premium income but cap your upside if the stock rises above the strike. This is one of the most popular income strategies — you're essentially renting out your shares.
maxLoss: Stock Price − Premium (if stock goes to $0)
idealCondition: You own stock, expect it to stay flat or rise slightly, and want income

name: Protective Put (Married Put)
description: Own 100 shares and buy a put as insurance. If the stock crashes, the put limits your downside. You keep all the upside minus the cost of the put. Think of it like buying insurance on your house — you pay a premium for protection.
idealCondition: You're bullish long-term but want protection against a short-term drop

name: Cash-Secured Put
description: Sell a put while holding enough cash to buy the stock if assigned. Identical payoff to a short put, but you're prepared to own the stock. Popular strategy to get paid while waiting to buy a stock at a lower price.
idealCondition: You want to buy a stock at a lower price and get paid to wait

name: Bull Call Spread
description: Buy a lower-strike call and sell a higher-strike call (same expiration). This reduces your cost compared to a long call, but caps your profit at the higher strike. Both your risk and reward are defined — you know the worst and best case upfront.
idealCondition: Moderately bullish — you expect the stock to rise but not skyrocket

name: Bull Put Spread
description: Sell a higher-strike put and buy a lower-strike put (same expiration). You collect a net credit upfront. You profit if the stock stays above the higher strike. This is a credit spread — you get paid to enter the trade.
idealCondition: Moderately bullish — you expect the stock to stay above the higher strike

name: Bear Call Spread
description: Sell a lower-strike call and buy a higher-strike call (same expiration). You collect a net credit. You profit if the stock stays below the lower strike. The bought call caps your risk if you're wrong.
idealCondition: Moderately bearish — you expect the stock to stay below the lower strike

name: Bear Put Spread
description: Buy a higher-strike put and sell a lower-strike put (same expiration). You pay a net debit. You profit if the stock falls below the higher strike, with max profit at or below the lower strike.
idealCondition: Moderately bearish — you expect the stock to decline but not crash

name: Protective Collar
description: Own stock + buy a put (protection) + sell a call (income). The call premium offsets the put cost, making the hedge cheap or free. Tradeoff: you cap your upside. Popular with investors who want to protect gains without selling.
idealCondition: You own stock with gains and want cheap downside protection

name: Long Straddle
description: Buy a call AND a put at the same strike price and expiration. You profit if the stock makes a big move in either direction. You lose if it stays near the strike. Great before earnings announcements or major events.
idealCondition: You expect a big move but don't know which direction

name: Short Straddle
description: Sell a call AND a put at the same strike. You collect both premiums and profit if the stock stays near the strike. WARNING: Unlimited risk in both directions. This is a bet on low volatility.
idealCondition: You expect the stock to stay flat with low volatility

name: Long Strangle
description: Buy an OTM call and an OTM put (different strikes, same expiration). Cheaper than a straddle but needs a bigger move to profit. You're betting on a large move in either direction.
breakeven: Lower Strike − Total Premium OR Upper Strike + Total Premium
idealCondition: You expect a very large move but want to spend less than a straddle

name: Short Strangle
description: Sell an OTM call and an OTM put (different strikes). You collect both premiums and profit if the stock stays between the two strikes. Wider profit zone than a short straddle, but less premium collected. WARNING: Unlimited risk.
breakeven: Lower Strike − Total Premium OR Upper Strike + Total Premium
idealCondition: You expect the stock to stay in a range with low volatility

name: Iron Condor
description: Sell an OTM put spread + sell an OTM call spread. You collect a net credit and profit if the stock stays between the two short strikes. All four legs define your max risk. This is one of the most popular income strategies for neutral markets.
breakeven: Lower short strike − Net Premium AND Upper short strike + Net Premium
idealCondition: You expect the stock to stay in a range with declining volatility

name: Reverse Iron Condor
description: Buy an OTM put spread + buy an OTM call spread. The opposite of an iron condor — you pay a net debit and profit if the stock makes a big move in either direction past the long strikes. Defined risk on both sides.
breakeven: Lower long strike − Net Premium AND Upper long strike + Net Premium
idealCondition: You expect a big move but want defined risk (cheaper than a strangle)

name: Iron Butterfly
description: Sell an ATM straddle + buy OTM wings (a put below and a call above). Like an iron condor but with the short strikes at the same price. Higher premium collected but a narrower profit zone. Max profit if stock expires exactly at the short strike.
idealCondition: You expect the stock to stay very close to the current price

name: Long Call Butterfly
description: Buy 1 ITM call, sell 2 ATM calls, buy 1 OTM call. All same expiration, equally spaced strikes. Max profit if stock expires exactly at the middle strike. Very low cost, very defined risk. A precision bet on a specific price.
breakeven: Lower Strike + Net Premium AND Upper Strike − Net Premium
idealCondition: You expect the stock to land near a specific price at expiration

name: Long Put Butterfly
description: Buy 1 OTM put, sell 2 ATM puts, buy 1 ITM put. Same structure as call butterfly but using puts. Identical payoff profile. Max profit at the middle strike.
breakeven: Lower Strike + Net Premium AND Upper Strike − Net Premium
idealCondition: You expect the stock to land near a specific price at expiration

name: Call Ratio Spread
description: Buy 1 lower-strike call, sell 2 higher-strike calls. Can be entered for a credit or small debit. Profits if stock rises moderately to the short strike. WARNING: Unlimited risk above the upper breakeven — the extra short call is naked.
breakeven: Lower Strike + Net Premium AND Higher Strike + Max Profit
idealCondition: Moderately bullish — you expect the stock to rise to but not far past the short strike

name: Put Ratio Spread
description: Buy 1 higher-strike put, sell 2 lower-strike puts. Profits if stock falls moderately. WARNING: Significant risk below the lower breakeven — the extra short put is naked.
breakeven: Higher Strike − Net Premium AND Lower Strike − Max Profit
idealCondition: Moderately bearish — you expect a decline but not a crash

name: Call Backspread
description: Sell 1 lower-strike call, buy 2 higher-strike calls. The opposite of a ratio spread. You want a big move up. If the stock barely moves, you lose. If it rockets, you profit from the extra long call.
maxLoss: (Higher Strike − Lower Strike) − Net Credit (at higher strike at expiration)
idealCondition: Strongly bullish — you expect a large upward move

name: Put Backspread
description: Sell 1 higher-strike put, buy 2 lower-strike puts. You want a big move down. Profits accelerate as the stock crashes because you hold 2 long puts against 1 short.
maxProfit: Substantial (2 long puts profit as stock falls to $0)
maxLoss: (Higher Strike − Lower Strike) − Net Credit (at lower strike at expiration)
idealCondition: Strongly bearish — you expect a crash or large decline

name: Synthetic Long Stock
description: Buy a call and sell a put at the same strike and expiration. This mimics owning 100 shares of stock but with much less capital. The payoff line looks identical to owning stock. Used for leverage or when you can't buy the stock directly.
maxLoss: Strike − Net Premium (same as stock going to $0)
idealCondition: Bullish — you want stock-like exposure with less capital

name: Synthetic Short Stock
description: Sell a call and buy a put at the same strike and expiration. This mimics shorting 100 shares without borrowing stock. The payoff is identical to a short stock position.
maxProfit: Strike − Net Premium (if stock goes to $0)
idealCondition: Bearish — you want short exposure without borrowing shares

name: Jade Lizard
description: Sell a put + sell a call spread (sell lower call, buy higher call). Structured so the total credit received exceeds the call spread width, eliminating upside risk. You only have downside risk. A creative income strategy.
idealCondition: Bullish/neutral — you want income with no upside risk
